coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots as “on-off-on” fluorescence probes to detect Hg2+ and MnO4 − and improving the photostability of Rhodamine B
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Wensu
Zhang, Shurong
Wang, Guiqiao
Cui, Jinzhi
Lu, Yaxin
Rong, Xing
Luo, Yawen
Zhang, LiChao
Cheng, Zhongfa
Gao, Canzhu
coredata.dc:description:
Background Carbon quantum dot (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm CQDs are widely used in the field of ion detection by virtue of their fluorescence characteristics such as strong fluorescence intensity, good optical stability and tunable emission wavelength. Although the traditional atomic absorption method, electrochemical method and other metal ion detection methods are highly sensitive, the operation is complex, expensive and limited by the site. Therefore, we prepared the N, S-CQDs capable of detecting Hg2+ and MnO4 − in water with the advantages of simple operation, low cost, and direct visual signal.
Results N, S-CQDs with high-quantum yield (77.68%), uniform particle size (0.4 nm–2.6 nm) and green fluorescence were created utilizing a one-pot hydrothermal process with the precursors ASDA-Na4 and m-phenylenediamine. N, S-CQDs has good optical properties such as high fluorescence intensity, wavelength independence, up-conversion luminescence and fluorescence stability. We examined 27 common ions in water and found that the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs could be selectively quenched by Hg2+ and MnO4 −, and the detection limits are 0.41 μM and 1.2 μM, respectively. The mechanism of quenching is further investigated. The fluorescence of N, S-CQDs-Hg2+ system can be restored by halogen ions (Cl−, Br−, I−), while the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs-MnO4 - system can be partially restored by Fe2+. This forms an “on-off-on” mode of fluorescent probes. In addition, we also studied that trace amounts of N, S-CQDs can improve the photostability of RhB.
Significance The N, S-CQDs are fluorescent probes in an “on-off-on” mode. N, S-CQDs with green fluorescence (on) can be quenched by Hg2+ and MnO4 − (off). The fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ can be restored by halogen ions again, while the fluorescence quenched by MnO4 − can partially be restored (on). This ion detection method can be used to visually detect the two ions in the field, with the advantages of low cost, simple operation and visual intuition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N
S-CQDs
Fluorescence sensor
Ion detection
Light stability

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst misctext primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Research on the photostability of RhB
Detection of Hg2+ and MnO4 − by N, S-CQDs
Improving the light stability of RhB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image with insets of particle size distribution, (b) XRD image, (c) FT-IR spectrum, (d) XPS full spectrum, (e) C1s XPS, (f) N1s XPS, (g) O1s XPS, (h) S2p XPS of N, S-CQDs.

(a) Ultraviolet absorption (black line), excitation (blue line), emission (red line) spectra (inset: N, S-CQDs solution under sunlight and ultraviolet lamp irradiation), (b) three-dimensional fluorescence spectra, (c) fluorescence emission spectra at 400 nm–490 nm excitation wavelength, (d) fluorescence emission wavelength at 800 nm–900 nm excitation wavelength of N, S-CQDs, (e) shelf-time, (f) ultraviolet irradiation time, (g) concentration of H2O2, (h) effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs solution in the presence of different ions, (b) fluorescence spectra, (c) fluorescence quenching ratio ((F0–F)/F0) of N, S-CQDs solution after adding different concentrations of Hg2+, (d) fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs-Hg2+ solution after adding different ions, (e) fluorescence spectra, (f) fluorescence quenching ratio ((F0–F)/F0) of N, S-CQDs solution after adding different concentrations of MnO4 −, (g) fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs-MnO4 - solution after adding different ions.

(a) Fluorescence attenuation trace, (b) absorption spectrum of N, S-CQDs, N, S-CQDs-Hg2+, N, S-CQDs-KMnO4, (c) Emission of N, S-CQDs and absorption spectrum of KMnO4, (d) FL intensity of N, S-CQDs and N, S-CQDs + MnO2, (e) absorption spectrum of N, S-CQDs, N, S-CQDs-Hg2+, N, S-CQDs-Hg2++ halogen ion.

(a) The effect of different concentrations of N, S-CQDs on the photodegradation of RhB, (b) the ultraviolet absorption spectrum and emission of N, S-CQDs and the absorption spectrum of RhB, (c) emission spectra of FRET system for N, S-CQDs-RhB.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots as “on-off-on” fluorescence probes to detect Hg2+ and MnO4 − and improving the photostability of Rhodamine B

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Taihe Technologies Co, Ltd, Zaozhuang, Shandong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Taihe Technologies Co, Ltd. Zaozhuang, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dot (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm CQDs are widely used in the field of ion detection by virtue of their fluorescence characteristics such as strong fluorescence intensity, good optical stability and tunable emission wavelength. Although the traditional atomic absorption method, electrochemical method and other metal ion detection methods are highly sensitive, the operation is complex, expensive and limited by the site. Therefore, we prepared the N, S-CQDs capable of detecting Hg2+ and MnO4 − in water with the advantages of simple operation, low cost, and direct visual signal.

N, S-CQDs with high-quantum yield (77.68%), uniform particle size (0.4 nm–2.6 nm) and green fluorescence were created utilizing a one-pot hydrothermal process with the precursors ASDA-Na4 and m-phenylenediamine. N, S-CQDs has good optical properties such as high fluorescence intensity, wavelength independence, up-conversion luminescence and fluorescence stability. We examined 27 common ions in water and found that the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs could be selectively quenched by Hg2+ and MnO4 −, and the detection limits are 0.41 μM and 1.2 μM, respectively. The mechanism of quenching is further investigated. The fluorescence of N, S-CQDs-Hg2+ system can be restored by halogen ions (Cl−, Br−, I−), while the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs-MnO4 - system can be partially restored by Fe2+. This forms an “on-off-on” mode of fluorescent probes. In addition, we also studied that trace amounts of N, S-CQDs can improve the photostability of RhB.

The N, S-CQDs are fluorescent probes in an “on-off-on” mode. N, S-CQDs with green fluorescence (on) can be quenched by Hg2+ and MnO4 − (off). The fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ can be restored by halogen ions again, while the fluorescence quenched by MnO4 − can partially be restored (on). This ion detection method can be used to visually detect the two ions in the field, with the advantages of low cost, simple operation and visual intuition.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon, as an important constituent element of living organisms, exists widely in various substances on earth. In 2004, xu et al. [1] discovered a kind of fluorescent carbon nano-particles during the electrophoresis-based separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes. In 2006, sun et al. [2] used laser etching of carbon targets to obtain carbon nanoparticles with fluorescent properties again and designated them as carbon dots. Carbon dots are biological zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures that resemble spheres and are less than 10 nm, and carbon quantum dot belongs to one kind of carbon dot [3]. The raw materials for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots are inexpensive and abundant, such as aloe vera [4], citric acid [5], graphite [6], etc. And there are various synthesis methods, such as hydrothermal method [7], solvothermal method [8], electrochemical method [9], etc. CQDs have fluorescence properties such as strong fluorescence intensity, great light stability, and adjustable emission wavelength [10], as well as the benefits of low toxicity, good water solubility and high biocompatibility [11]. The core of carbon quantum dots is mainly graphitized sp2 hybridized carbon, and the surface is rich in –COOH, –OH and other groups [12]. It can be doped with other heteroatoms (such as N [13], S [14], P [15], etc.) for surface passivation, which makes the surface functional groups more abundant and can be easily combined with other materials and used in many fields, such as photocatalysis [16], bioimaging [17], electrochemistry [18], biosensing [19], etc. Because of these excellent properties, carbon quantum dots have another important application, that is, as fluorescent probes for the analysis and detection of targets [20]. More and more articles are using CQDs to detect metal ions, anions, organic compounds [21] in the environment and for biomedical and food analysis.

With the development of industry, heavy metal ions in domestic water have caused a severe threat to people's health and environment. Several heavy metal ions are extremely poisonous or cancer-causing. Mercury is one of the most poisonous heavy metal ions, which mainly harms the human central nervous system, causing brain damage and mercury poisoning. In addition, mercury is transformed in the body into the more toxic methylmercury [22]. Manganese causes blood vessel congestion and myocardial infarction in living organisms and also affects cell viability [23]. Therefore, it is essential to detect mercury and manganese in aqueous solutions. Although the traditional atomic absorption method, electrochemical method, atomic emission spectrometry and other metal ion detection methods are highly sensitive, the operation is complex, expensive and limited by the site. However, the detection of metal ions using CQDs as fluorescent probes has the advantages of simple operation, low cost and direct visual signal [24].

Rhodamine B (RhB) is a red synthetic dye commonly used as a fluorescent cell stain in the laboratory, the paper industry, the manufacture of paints and applications in tinted glass and other industries. However, RhB will gradually fade under light (UV and visible light), because the structure of the dye molecules will be damaged after absorbing a certain number of photons, which is a great disadvantage for the dye. We found that adding trace amounts of N, S-CQDs can resist the degradation of RhB by high-energy ultraviolet light. This will provide a new idea for the dye industry in the future.

In recent years, more and more scholars have started to study N, S, and P doped CQDs to detect heavy metal ions in water [25,26]. Omer et al. [27] prepared N, P doped CQDs by hydrothermal method, which could detect Cr(Ⅱ) in water and the quantum yield was 0.41. The green fluorescent probes (B, N-CQDs) prepared by zhao et al. [28] were able to simultaneously detect Co(Ⅱ) in environmental water, human urine and cells with a detection limit of 0.52 nM. However, most of the CQDs as fluorescent probes can only detect one or one type of metal ions, while the detection of two heavy metal ions is less studied. Such CQDs can be expanded in the application of sensors. In this paper, using ASDA-Na4 and m-phenylenediamine as precursors, CQDs (N, S-CQDs) containing N, S co-doping were constructed in one step by hydrothermal method for the detection of Hg2+ and MnO4 −. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were used to observe and study the sizes and morphology of N, S-CQDs. We studied the detection limits of N, S-CQDs and the influence of other ions on the detection of Hg2+ and MnO4 −, and found that halogen ion (Cl−, Br−, I−) can restore fluorescence of N, S-CQDs after Hg2+ quenching, while MnO4 − cannot. It is proposed in this work that trace amounts of N, S-CQDs can enhance the photostability of the dye.

Tetrasodium Dicarboxymethyl Aspartate (ASDA-Na4, ≥55%) was purchased from Jining Yuanlian Chemical Technology Co., LTD. (Shandong, China). m-phenylenediamine (MPD, C6H8N2, 99%), FeCl3 6H2O, CuCl2 2H2O, MnSO4 H2O, CdCl2 2.5H2O, HgSO4, NiSO4 6H2O, AlCl3, Co(CH3COO)2, AgNO3, KI, KBr and ZnCl2 were acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HCl, HNO3, H2SO4, MgCl2, KCl, CaCl2, BaCl2 2H2O, NaOH, NaCl, Na3PO4 12H2O, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NH4Cl, HClO, HClO4, KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used for each experiment.

N, S-CQDs were fabricated in one step by a typical hydrothermal method [29] using ASDA-Na4 and m-phenylenediamine as precursors. First, 0.5 mmol of ASDA-Na4 and 1.5 mmol of MPD were dispersed in 20 ml of deionized water (ASDA-Na4/MPD molar ratio was 1:3). After mixing well, add 5 mM H2SO4 to adjust pH to about 4.0. The mixture was stirred magnetically for 10 min and moved to a 50 cc Teflon-lined reactor and heated for 9 h at 190 °C. The solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 da) for 24 h after being cooled to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter. Finally, a clarified brown liquid was obtained, which was freeze-dried to a powder for further characterization and reserve. The powder was dissolved in deionized water as a stock solution (0.5 g/L).

The morphological characteristics of N, S-CQDs were obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan) and their particle size analysis was measured by “Nano measure”. The chemical components of N, S-CQDs was assayed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo scientific K-Alpha, America). The structures of N, S-CQDs were studied using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo IN10, USA) was used to determine the surface functional groups and chemical composition of the N, S-CQDs. An ultraviolet visible spectrometer (UV–Vis, UV-2450, Japan) and a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan) from Agilent were used to test the optical properties of CQDs. The fluorescence lifetimes were determined by transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometry (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK).

A reference method [30] was used to determine QY. Rhodamine 6G (R6G) solved in ethanol was selected as the reference solvent. The QY of R6G at the optimal excitation wavelength of 440 nm was 0.95. In order to minimize the effect of self-absorption effect, the absorbance of both substances was kept below 0.1. N, S-CQDs is calculated by the formula QY
1
= QY
0
× (F
1
/F
0
) × (A
0
/A
1
) × (η
2
1
/η
2
0
). Where “1”, “0” represents N, S-CQDs, R6G, respectively, “F” means integrated luminescence intensity, “A” refers to absorbance, and “η” reflects index of refraction. Water has a refractive index of 1.33 and ethanol has a refractive index of 1.36.

In the fluorescence detection experiment, N, S-CQDs was diluted with Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH = 5) and configured as N,S-CQDs reserve solution of 5 mg/L. Preparation of various detection ion solutions (0.1 M, Ba2+, Na+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, NH4 +, MnO4 −, PO4 3−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, ClO−, CrO4 2−, Cr2O7 2−, Cl−, Br−, I−). Equal amounts of ion solutions were added to equal amounts of N, S-CQDs stock solution, respectively. After mixing the reaction for 30 min, the fluorescence was analyzed at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm. It was found that only Hg2+ and MnO4 − were available to quench the green fluorescence. Subsequently, the effects of ion concentration on fluorescence quenching were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on the photostability of RhB
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment to verify that N, S-CQDs can improve the photostability of RhB, three samples of each concentration were prepared to reduce the error. The concentration of the dye was set at 5 ppm and the concentrations of N, S-CQDs were 0,1,3,5,8,10,15 ppm, respectively. The mixed solutions were placed on a magnetic stirrer for 30 min for the dark reaction and then placed in a UV disinfection device, followed by irradiation with a 254 nm UV lamp. Finally, the degradation of RhB at different times was recorded.

To optimize the composite process of N, S-CQDs, the effects of ratio, temperature, time, pH and concentration on their fluorescence intensity were researched. Firstly, the ratio of ASDA-Na4 and MPD was optimized. When all other conditions were the same, it was found that the optimal emission intensity of N, S-CQDs was achieved at the molar ratio of ASDA-Na4: MPD of 1:3. The preparation temperature of N, S-CQDs was tested from 170 °C to 220 °C. As shown in Fig. S1b, the fluorescence was strongest at the carbonization temperature of 190 °C. In a certain range, the degree of carbonization increases with the increase of temperature, but the agglomeration and oxidation of N, S-CQDs will be caused when the temperature is too high. Then the reaction time and pH were changed respectively. As shown in Figs. S1c–d, the fluorescence intensity showed the best at 9 h and pH = 4. Finally, the concentration of the two substances was regulated, and it was found that the optimal concentration of ASDA-Na4 was 0.5 mmol. When the concentration was too high, the fluorescence intensity was affected by insufficient carbonization instead. N, S-CQDs are produced under these conditions with a luminescence yield of 77.68%, which is better than that of carbon quantum dots synthesized previously reported (see Table S1 for details).

Fig. 1 a shows the TEM images of N, S-CQDs, and it can be seen that the N, S-CQDs are relatively spherically complete, homogeneous in size and well dispersed. In order to understand the particle size and distribution of N, S-CQDs, the nano measure software was used to conduct statistics on 100 randomly selected dots. The inset shows that the particle size of N, S-CQDs ranges from 0.4 nm to 2.6 nm, and the average particle size is 1.43 nm. The tiny size of N, S-CQDs predicts a high degree of carbonization [31] and no agglomeration [32] during the synthesis process. In the XRD pattern of N, S-CQDs (Fig. 1b), a wide diffraction peak with 2θ = 23.56° as the center can be observed, which illustrates the amorphous carbon structure of N, S-CQDs [33]. In addition, sharp peaks appear at 31.8° and 33.95°, and this may be a disordered structure of graphitic carbon, where the carbon atoms may be present as alkyl chains. This also demonstrates the low crystallinity of the carbon point, which may be related to the high number of functional groups. As seen in Fig. 1c, the peaks at 3332.84 cm−1 and 3206.03 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, suggesting the presence of a large number of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N, S-CQDs [34,35]. The absorption peak at 2916.50 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of C–H. The narrow absorption peaks at 1625.73 cm−1 and 1496.75 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CC/CO and CN [36]. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1379.89 cm−1 and 1318.39 cm−1 are due to C–N [37]. The narrow absorption peak at 1139.52 cm−1 is associated with CS and SO vibrational stretching [38] and the sharp peak at 621.74 cm−1 is because of the presence of C–S [39]. In order to explore the surface element composition of N, S-CQD, XPS analysis was carried out. It can be seen from the XPS total spectrum that there are four peaks at 168.4 eV, 284.8 eV, 399.3 eV and 531.6 eV, corresponding to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s respectively (Fig. 1d). It can be seen from the illustrations that the content of C, O, N and S elements accounted for 68.61%, 17.92%, 11.21% and 2.27%, respectively (Fig. 1d). By analyzing the peak spectrum of each element, the chemical valence distribution of N, S-CQDs elements was clearly understood. By peak fitting, it was found that C1s had three fitting peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV and 287.6 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O and CO respectively [40], among which C–C/CC accounted for the highest proportion (Fig. 1e). This also verifies the sp2 graphitic CC conjugated structure of N, S-CQDs. There are two fitted peaks in the high-resolution spectrum of N1s, which are C–N and C–NH2, respectively (Fig. 1f). The two fitted peaks at 531.98 eV and 533.5 eV in the spectrum of O1s indicate that N, S-CQDs have high oxidation states (Fig. 1g). It can be seen from Fig. 1h that the two signals of S2p at 167.3 eV and 168.6 eV may belong to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of C-Sox [41].

As shown in the inset of Fig. 2 a, the aqueous solution of N, S-CQDs showed a light yellow color under sunlight and green fluorescence under UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm, which proved that the N, S-CQDs prepared by hydrothermal method had fluorescent properties. To further explore the fluorescence features of N, S-CQDs, their UV–visible and fluorescence spectrums were studied. It can be seen from Fig. 2a that there are multiple absorption peaks between 200 nm and 600 nm. The conjugate CC bond's π-π* transition is what causes the strong absorption peak at 219 nm and the acromion at 260 nm. And the wide peaks at 400 nm–500 nm are due to the n-π* transition of the CO bond. The measured fluorescence spectra show that the maximum excitation wavelength of N, S-CQDs is 440 nm and the maximum emission wavelength is 515 nm. Fig. 2b shows the three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of N, S-CQDs, from which it can be seen that the PL excitation range of N, S-CQDs is roughly between 360 nm and 460 nm, and fluorescence emission exists in the wavelength range between 460 nm and 580 nm. Of course, the emission fluorescence spectra of N, S-CQDs at various excitation wavelengths were also explored. As the excitation wavelength progressively shifts from 400 nm to 490 nm, the fluorescence intensity initially increases, and decreases after increasing to a certain level (Fig. 2c). However, the emission wavelength does not vary with the excitation wavelength, which shows that the excitation wavelength is independent [34]. This indicates that the synthesized N, S-CQDs is relatively stable. In addition, the N, S-CQDs show the fluorescence characteristic of rare carbon quantum dots — up-conversion luminescence [42]. When N, S-CQDs is excited by 800 nm–900 nm light, the emission peak is still around 515 nm. This implies that N, S-CQDs can absorb long-wavelength, low-energy light to produce short-wavelength, high-energy light. Of course, this statement is still controversial. The effects of shelving time, UV lamp irradiation time, hydrogen peroxide concentration and pH on the fluorescence stability of N, S-CQDs were discussed further. It is shown that (1) the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs solution stored for four weeks was only slightly reduced, (2) the fluorescence intensity remained undiminished after 90 min of UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm, and (3) the reducing H2O2 also had no impact on the luminescence of N, S-CQDs, (4) but the pH had a strong effect on the fluorescence intensity, and N, S-CQDs showed good fluorescence intensity in weak acidity (pH = 3–6), while the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs was unfavorable in both neutral and basic. The fluorescence intensity was optimum when pH = 5 (Fig. 2e-h). Therefore, in the following ion detection, the solution was stabilized at pH = 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and MnO4 − by N, S-CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: N, S-CQDs are frequently applied as fluorescent sensors for heavy metal ions because of their outstanding optical properties. Therefore, excellent selectivity is an essential indicator of the photosensor. Ba2+, Na+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, NH4 + cations and MnO4 −, PO4 3−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, ClO−, CrO4 2−, Cr2O7 2−, Cl−, Br−, I− anions were evaluated for N, S-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence degree of solution with N, S-CQDs added with Hg2+ and MnO4 − is significantly reduced, while other ions have only small impact on its fluorescence intensity, which proves that N, S-CQDs has good chemical stability and excellent selectivity. Fig. S2 shows the photographs of the solutions of N, S-CQDs under the irradiation of 365 nm handheld UV lamp after the addition of different ions. It can be seen that the samples with the addition of Hg2+ and MnO4 − have basically no fluorescence. The above results demonstrate that N, S-CQDs can be selectively turned off (off) by Hg2+ and MnO4 − very effectively. In addition, N, S-CQDs quenched by Hg2+ were able to recover fluorescence selectively by Cl−, Br−, and I−. And the fluorescence quenched by MnO4 − can be partially restored by Fe2+. In this way, an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+ and MnO4 − was established.

Based on the good selectivity of N, S-CQDs for the detection of trace Hg2+, the effect of different concentrations (0 μM–120 μM) of Hg2+ on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs was further conducted. It was discovered that as the quantity of Hg2+ increased, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs dropped. ((on-off) Fig. 3b). A linear fit was performed with the relative fluorescence intensity ((F0–F)/F0) of the N, S-CQDs solution as the vertical coordinate and the concentration of Hg2+ as the horizontal coordinate (Fig. 3c). When the Hg2+ concentration is between 0 μM and 30 μM, there is a solid linear relationship. The linear equation is Y = −0.0135 + 0.0103X, and the linear relation R2 = 0.985(Fig. 3c. Inset). In addition, the minimum detection limit L = 0.41 μM was calculated by the equation LOD = 3σ/slope (where the signal-to-noise ratio was 3). We also explored the fluorescence recovery of the N, S-CQDs-Hg2+ system. Equal amounts of Ba2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, NH4 + cations and PO4 3−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, ClO−, CrO4 2−, Cr2O7 2−, Cl−, Br−, I− anions were added to the solution of N, S-CQDs after quenching by Hg2+. It can be seen from Fig. 3d that the addition of Cl−, Br−, and I− ions can restore the fluorescence to different degrees, with I− basically reaching the fluorescence intensity of the original N, S-CQDs. However, the addition of other ions did not restore their fluorescence. The addition of Cl−, Br−, and I− did not quench the N, S-CQDs solution, but the fluorescence was restored for the N, S-CQDs-Hg2+ system (off-on).

By maintaining a constant concentration of N, S-CQDs, the impact of varying MnO4 − (0 μM–50 μM) concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs was investigated. N, S-CQDs fluorescence emission and MnO4 −concentration are tightly correlated. N, S-CQD emission intensity decreases as MnO4 − concentration rises ((on-off) Fig. 3e). By linear fitting of (F0–F)/F0–MnO4 - concentration, the linear relationship is satisfactory between 0 μM and 20 μM (Fig. 3f). The linear regression equation is Y = −0.0231 + 0.024X, the linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.991, and the detection limit is 1.2 μM. The fluorescence restoration experiment of N, S-CQDs-MnO4 - system was also carried out. An equal number of Ba2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, NO3 −, S2O3 2−, Cl−, Br−, I− ions are added to the N, S-CQDs-MnO4 - system. After standing for a period of time, the average fluorescence intensity of three times was determined. It can be seen from Fig. 3g that Fe2+ and S2O3 2− can make N, S-CQDs-MnO4 - recover weakly (off-on). Fe2+ only restored the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs-MnO4 - to 1/5 of the original, while halogen elements could not restore its fluorescence.

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching mainly includes static quenching, dynamic quenching, internal filtration effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer [43]. Dynamic quenching refers to the effect of energy transfer or charge transfer resulting in fluorescence weakening due to the collision between N, S-CQDs and quencher, which will shorten the fluorescence lifetime. Static quenching is the interaction between N, S-CQDs and quenchers to form non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent material with little change in the lifetime of the excited state of the N, S-CQDs. The existence of a duplication of the N, S-CQDs' emission spectrum and the receptor's absorption spectrum is a prerequisite for the fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The internal filtration effect is a phenomenon when an excess of N, S-CQDs or quenchers in the sensing system is capable of absorbing the excitation or emission light of N, S-CQDs, causing a weakening of the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 4 a shows the fluorescence decay traces of N, S-CQDs before and after quenching, and the fluorescence lifetimes were calculated by fitting to 7.230 ns (N, S-CQDs), 7.123 ns (N, S-CQDs + Hg2+) and 7.141 ns (N, S-CQDs + MnO4 −), respectively. It can be seen that the fluorescence lifetimes of N, S-CQDs were not shortened by the addition of Hg2+ and MnO4 −, indicating that the quenching process was not dynamic quenching but probably static quenching. Next, the UV absorption spectra of N, S-CQDs before and after quenching by Hg2+ were measured. As can be seen in Fig. 4b, the absorption peaks of N, S-CQDs at 400 nm–450 nm were weakened after the addition of Hg2+, while the absorption peaks between 300 nm and 400 nm were enhanced. This explains that the addition of Hg2+ produced new substances [44]. The same is true for the addition of MnO4 −, where a new peak appears at 350 nm, which also confirms the generation of new substances. This further proves the principle of static quenching. The absorption spectra of MnO4 − and the emission spectra of N, S-CQDs appear to be highly overlapping between 450 nm and 600 nm (Fig. 4c), implying that the addition of MnO4 − might cause fluorescence resonance energy transfer or internal filtration effect of this system. This reasoning also needs further verification. Another possibility is that MnO2 is produced during the reaction of KMnO4, and MnO2 is reported to quench the fluorescence of CQDs [45]. To verify this hypothesis, a certain amount of KMnO4 and excess MnSO4·H2O were dissolved in 50 ml deionized water and heated in a water bath for 2 h at 90 °C, stirring constantly. In this way, MnO2 nanoparticles are prepared. Quantitative MnO2 was added into N, S-CQDs solution, and it was found that fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4d). In addition to this, the mechanism of fluorescence restoration in the N, S-CQDs-Hg2+ system after the addition of halogen ions was also tested. As shown in Fig. 4e, the absorption peaks of the N, S-CQDs-Hg2+ system located between 400 nm and 450 nm reappeared after the addition of halogen ions. And the absorption peak of N, S-CQDs-Hg2+-I- almost reached the height of the absorption peak of N, S-CQDs, while the absorption peak of N, S-CQDs-Hg2+-Cl- was the lowest. This also confirms the extent of the recovered fluorescence in Fig. 3d. Therefore, it is speculated that the mechanism of fluorescence recovery is that the binding force between halogen ions and Hg2+ is stronger than that between N, S-CQDs and Hg2+. As for Fe2+, which can only restore partial fluorescence of N, S-CQDs-KMnO4 system, the specific mechanism reaction is as follows: KMnO4 has strong oxidation and is easy to absorb electrons. KMnO4 interacts with N, S-CQDs surface groups [46], destroying some functional groups and fluorescent chromophores. The remaining fraction combines with the surface of N, S-CQDs to form a new material that does not emit light. When Fe2+ is added, KMnO4 bound to N, S-CQDs rapidly reacts with strongly reducing Fe2+, but those groups and chromophores that have been destroyed cannot be regenerated, and thus fluorescence is only partially restored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improving the light stability of RhB
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Fig. 5 a, with the increase of N, S-CQDs concentration, the degradation rate of RhB under 254 nm UV lamp gradually decreases. In 120 min, the degradation rate of RhB reached 90% without the addition of N, S-CQDs. When adding 15 ppm N, S-CQDs, the degradation rate of Rh B reached 20%. This suggests that the addition of N, S-CQDs has a significant impact on RhB's photostability. Fig. 5b shows the UV absorption spectra, the fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CQDs, and the absorption spectra of RhB. We can see that the emission spectra curves of N, S-CQDs and the absorption curves of RhB overlap to a large extent. This indicates that there may be fluorescence resonance energy transfer between N, S-CQDs and RhB [45]. To verify this speculation, we examined the fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CQDs, RhB and N, S-CQDs-RhB at the same excitation wavelength. It can be seen from Fig. 5c that the fluorescence emission spectrum of N, S-CQDs-RhB shows two peaks at 515 nm and 579 nm. The fluorescence peak of N, S-CQDs-RhB at 515 nm was weakened compared with that of N, S-CQDs alone, however, the fluorescence peak of N, S-CQDs-RhB at 579 nm was significantly enhanced in comparison with that of RhB alone. This proves that N, S-CQDs act as a donor to transfer energy to RhB acceptor by non-radiative resonance of dipole bonding [47], and thus are not easily degraded by UV light. However, this mechanism still needs further investigation.

This study describes the hydrothermal synthesis of N, S-CQDs with green fluorescence doped with both N and S elements utilizing ASDA-Na4 and m-phenylenediamine as precursors. The N, S-CQDs have good water dispersion, uniform particle size distribution (0.4 nm–2.6 nm), high quantum yields (77.68%) and excellent optical properties. Most importantly, N, S-CQDs can be used as fluorescent probes for heavy metal ions. the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs can be selectively quenched by Hg2+ and MnO4 −. The fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ can be restored by halogen ions again, however, the fluorescence quenched by MnO4 − can hardly be restored. In addition, we found that trace amounts of N, S-CQDs can improve the photostability of dyes, which has potential applications in the dye industry.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Wensu Gao: Conceptualization, Sort out literature, Design, thesis framework, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shurong Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology. Guiqiao Wang: Writing – review & editing. Jinzhi Cui: Writing – review & editing. Yaxin Lu: Writing – review & editing. Xing Rong: Writing – review & editing. Yawen Luo: Writing – review & editing. LiChao Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Zhongfa Cheng: Writing – review & editing. Canzhu Gao: Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
